planting.note: Plant seeds in spring after cold stratification or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; eastern white pine grows naturally in a wide variety of well-drained, slightly acidic soils but will not tolerate salt spray, heavy clay, or compacted urban conditions. Avoid exposed, windy hilltops.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; eastern white pine is the largest native pine in the eastern US — straight, fast-growing, and graceful, with distinctive soft, blue-green needles in bundles of five and long, curved, resinous cones. At maturity, the old trees develop a characteristic flat, spreading, wind-sculptured crown that is one of the most distinctive silhouettes in the eastern American landscape. Historically one of the most important timber trees in North America — the tall, straight trunks were marked with the broad arrow as masts for the British Royal Navy, a source of colonial conflict. The flexible, soft needles are distinctive among eastern pines. Young trees make fast-growing screens and hedges when sheared. Particularly effective as a windbreak or background planting. Blister rust (Cronartium ribicola) is a serious disease threat in some areas; plant rust-resistant selections when available.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Japanese black pine is exceptionally salt- and wind-tolerant, making it the premier coastal landscape pine in the eastern US. It performs well in sandy, well-drained coastal soils but struggles in clay or poorly drained conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Japanese black pine is the premier coastal landscape pine in North America — extraordinarily salt-tolerant and wind-resistant, developing the dramatic, asymmetric, wind-sculpted forms characteristic of shoreline pines in its native Japan. The paired (two-needle) needles are dark green and stiff. Widely used in formal Japanese gardens, bonsai (where it is the premier subject for pine bonsai in Japanese tradition), and coastal landscapes from Cape Cod to the Gulf Coast. The aggressive spring candle growth is the focus of Japanese bonsai 'candle-cutting' technique, which stimulates dense, compact needle growth. Highly susceptible to Pine Wilt disease (Bursaphelenchus xylophilus nematode) in parts of the eastern US — check with local extension services for current disease pressure. The most widely planted Japanese pine in American landscapes.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; Japanese red pine grows naturally on dry, poor, rocky mountain slopes of Japan and Korea, adapting to lean, acidic, well-drained conditions. Avoid fertile, moist soils which cause excessive, rank growth.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; Japanese red pine (akamatsu, 'red pine') is celebrated in Japanese art and literature — the sinuous, red-barked old specimens on rocky mountain outcrops of Japan are among the most recognizable images in East Asian landscape painting. The orange-red, flaking bark on the upper trunk and branches is uniquely beautiful, deepening in color and contrast as the tree matures. The paired, soft, blue-green needles and naturally irregular, layered crown give the tree a relaxed, artistic quality that makes it the premier pine for Japanese-inspired landscape design. The cultivar 'Umbraculifera' (Tanyosho pine) is a dwarf, umbrella-shaped form widely used in Japanese gardens. The cultivar 'Oculus-Draconis' ('Dragon's Eye') has variegated needles with yellow bands. Somewhat susceptible to pine wilt nematode in the eastern US.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, dry to average, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; Aleppo pine is a Mediterranean dryland pine adapted to hot, dry, alkaline conditions and thin limestone or sandy soils. One of the most drought-tolerant pines for hot, dry climates in zones 8–10.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry to average, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; Aleppo pine is the most drought-tolerant and widely distributed pine of the Mediterranean Basin, covering rocky, thin limestone and sandy soils around the sea from Spain to Turkey. In California and the Southwest US, it has been planted for over a century as a tough, drought-tolerant pine for hot, dry, alkaline conditions where most other pines fail. The light gray-green to blue-green paired needles and the open, slightly irregular crown give it a soft, Mediterranean character. Old trees develop a picturesque, wind-sculpted silhouette with distinctive sinuous, upswept branches. The small, asymmetric, orange-brown cones are persistent and add winter interest. Well-suited to the dry, alkaline conditions of California's interior valleys, the Southwest, and the alkaline calcareous soils of the Hill Country and Edwards Plateau of Texas.

planting.note: Plant seeds after cold stratification in spring or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; sugar pine grows naturally in mixed conifer forests of the Sierra Nevada and Cascades at 2,000–10,000 feet elevation. Plant in a permanent site as transplanting established trees is very difficult.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; sugar pine is the largest and longest-coned pine in the world — individual trees reach 200–270 feet in height, and the cones (hanging from branch tips on stalks) measure 10–26 inches long, the longest cones of any conifer. John Muir called it 'the king of the conifers.' The species has the largest seed (pine nut) of any western pine, and the seeds were an important food source for Sierra Nevada Native peoples. The common name comes from the sweet, sugary sap or resin that exudes from wounds, tasting like brown sugar and used as a sweetener by indigenous peoples. Native to the Sierra Nevada and Cascade Range from Oregon through central California, the species is unfortunately threatened by white pine blister rust (Cronartium ribicola) — plant blister-rust-resistant selections when available. The five-needle bundles, massive trunk, and extraordinary long cones make it one of the most impressive trees in North America.
